section.title: The Book of Consolation
section.sectionRationale: In the middle of a book dominated by judgment, Jeremiah is told to write down a sustained oracle of pure hope: Jacob's distress will end in salvation, the broken nation will be rebuilt, mourning will turn to joy, and, at the very center of it all, God will make a new covenant, writing His law on hearts rather than tablets and forgiving sin once for all.
section.thesis: God's discipline of His people through exile is real and just, but it is not His last word, His unbreakable love guarantees a future restoration that culminates in a new covenant of inward transformation and full forgiveness, the very covenant Christ inaugurates at the Last Supper and the cross.
section.frameNote: Eight scenes: restoration promised amid the day of Jacob's trouble; an incurable wound that God alone heals; the city rebuilt under a ruler from their own midst; everlasting love rebuilding Virgin Israel; Rachel's weeping turned to hope; the land resettled and personal responsibility restored; the new covenant of the heart, the summit of the whole book; and God's covenant faithfulness as fixed as creation itself, sealed in a holy, rebuilt Jerusalem.

units.label: Restoration and the day of Jacob's trouble

verses.text: This is the word that came to Jeremiah from the LORD:
“This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: ‘Write in a book all the words that I have spoken to you.

For behold, the days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will restore from captivity My people Israel and Judah, declares the LORD. I will restore them to the land that I gave to their fathers, and they will possess it.’”
These are the words that the LORD spoke concerning Israel and Judah.

Yes, this is what the LORD says: 
“A cry of panic is heard, a cry of terror, not of peace.

Ask now, and see: Can a male give birth? Why then do I see every man with his hands on his stomach like a woman in labor 
and every face turned pale?
How awful that day will be! None will be like it! It is the time of Jacob's distress, but he will be saved out of it.

On that day, declares the LORD of Hosts, I will break the {{ol|yoke}} off their necks and tear off their bonds, 
and no longer will strangers enslave them.

Instead, they will serve the LORD their God and David their king, 
whom I will raise up for them.

As for you, O Jacob My servant, do not be afraid, declares the LORD, and do not be dismayed, O Israel. For I will surely save you out of a distant place, your descendants from the land of their captivity! Jacob will return to quiet and ease, with no one to make him afraid.

For I am with you to save you, declares the LORD. Though I will completely destroy all the nations to which I have scattered you, I will not completely destroy you. Yet I will discipline you justly, and will by no means leave you unpunished.”

units.label: An incurable wound, a faithful healer
units.note: who no one cares for, He does

verses.text:
For this is what the LORD says: 
“Your injury is incurable; your wound is grievous.

There is no one to plead your cause, no remedy for your sores, 
no recovery for you.
All your lovers have forgotten you; they no longer seek you, for I have struck you as an enemy would, with the discipline of someone cruel, because of your great iniquity and your numerous sins.

Why do you cry out over your wound? Your pain has no cure! Because of your great iniquity and your numerous sins 
I have done these things to you.

Nevertheless, all who devour you will be devoured, and all your adversaries, every one of them, will go off into exile. Those who plundered you will be plundered, and all who raided you will be raided.
But I will restore your health and heal your wounds, declares the LORD, because they call you an outcast, Zion, for whom no one cares.”

units.label: The city rebuilt, a ruler from their own midst

verses.text:
This is what the LORD says: 
“I will restore the fortunes of Jacob's tents and have compassion on his dwellings. And the city will be rebuilt on her own ruins, and the palace will stand in its rightful place.
Thanksgiving will proceed from them, a sound of celebration. I will multiply them, and they will not be decreased; I will honor them, and they will not be belittled.

Their children will be as in days of old, and their congregation will be established before Me; 
and I will punish all their oppressors.

Their leader will be one of their own, and their ruler will arise from their midst. And I will bring him near, and he will approach Me, for who would dare on his own to approach Me?” declares the LORD.
“And you will be My people, and I will be your God.”
Behold, the storm of the LORD has gone out with fury, a whirlwind swirling down upon the heads of the wicked.
The fierce anger of the LORD will not turn back until He has fully accomplished the purposes of His heart. In the days to come you will understand this.
“At that time,” declares the LORD, “I will be the God of all the families of Israel, and they will be My people.”

This is what the LORD says: 
“The people who survived the sword found favor in the wilderness 
when Israel went to find rest.”

The LORD appeared to us in the past, saying: 
“I have loved you with an {{ahavatOlam|everlasting love}}; therefore I have drawn you with loving devotion.
Again I will build you, and you will be rebuilt, O Virgin Israel. Again you will take up your tambourines and go out in joyful dancing.
Again you will plant vineyards on the hills of Samaria; the farmers will plant and enjoy the fruit.
For there will be a day when watchmen will call out on the hills of Ephraim, ‘Arise, let us go up to Zion, to the LORD our God!’”

For this is what the LORD says: 
“Sing with joy for Jacob; shout for the foremost of the nations! Make your praises heard, and say, ‘O LORD, save Your people, 
the remnant of Israel!’

Behold, I will bring them from the land of the north and gather them from the farthest parts of the earth, including the blind and the lame, expectant mothers and women in labor. 
They will return as a great assembly!

They will come with weeping, and by their supplication I will lead them; I will make them walk beside streams of waters, on a level path where they will not stumble. For I am Israel's Father, and Ephraim is My firstborn.”

Hear, O nations, the word of the LORD, and proclaim it in distant coastlands: 
“The One who scattered Israel will gather them and keep them as a shepherd keeps his flock.
For the LORD has ransomed Jacob and redeemed him from the hand that had overpowered him.

They will come and shout for joy on the heights of Zion; they will be radiant over the bounty of the LORD, the grain, new wine, and oil, and the young of the flocks and herds. Their life will be like a well-watered garden, and never again will they languish.
Then the maidens will rejoice with dancing, young men and old as well. I will turn their mourning into joy, and give them comfort and joy for their sorrow.
I will fill the souls of the priests abundantly, and will fill My people with My goodness,” declares the LORD.

This is what the LORD says: 
“A voice is heard in Ramah, mourning and great weeping, Rachel weeping for her children, and refusing to be comforted, 
because they are no more.”

This is what the LORD says: 
“Keep your voice from weeping and your eyes from tears, for the reward for your work will come, declares the LORD. Then your children will return from the land of the enemy.
So there is hope for your future, declares the LORD, and your children will return to their own land.

I have surely heard Ephraim's moaning: 
‘You disciplined me severely, like an untrained calf. Restore me, that I may return, for You are the LORD my God.
After I returned, I repented; and after I was instructed, I struck my thigh in grief. I was ashamed and humiliated because I bore the disgrace of my youth.’

Is not Ephraim a precious son to Me, a delightful child? Though I often speak against him, I still remember him. Therefore My heart yearns for him; I have great compassion for him,” declares the LORD.
“Set up the roadmarks, establish the signposts. Keep the highway in mind, the road you have traveled. Return, O Virgin Israel, return to these cities of yours.
How long will you wander, O faithless daughter? For the LORD has created a new thing in the land, a woman will shelter a man.”

units.label: The land resettled, sour grapes no more
units.note: each will die for his own iniquity

verses.text: This is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: “When I restore them from captivity, they will once again speak this word in the land of Judah and in its cities: ‘May the LORD bless you, O righteous dwelling place, O holy mountain.’
And Judah and all its cities will dwell together in the land, the farmers and those who move with the flocks,
for I will refresh the weary soul and replenish all who are weak.”
At this I awoke and looked around. My sleep had been most pleasant to me.
“The days are coming,” declares the LORD, “when I will sow the house of Israel and the house of Judah with the seed of man and of beast.
Just as I watched over them to uproot and tear down, to demolish, destroy, and bring disaster, so I will watch over them to build and to plant,” declares the LORD.

“In those days, it will no longer be said: 
‘The fathers have eaten sour grapes, and the teeth of the children are set on edge.’
Instead, each will die for his own iniquity. If anyone eats the sour grapes, his own teeth will be set on edge.

units.label: The new covenant
units.note: My law within them, written on their hearts

verses.text: Behold, the days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will make a {{beritChadashah|new covenant}} with the house of Israel and with the house of Judah.

It will not be like the covenant I made with their fathers when I took them by the hand to lead them out of the land of Egypt, a covenant they broke, though I was a husband to them,” declares the LORD.

“But this is the covenant I will make with the house of Israel after those days, declares the LORD. I will put My law in their minds and inscribe it on their hearts. And I will be their God, and they will be My people.

No longer will each man teach his neighbor or his brother, saying, ‘Know the LORD,’ because they will all {{yada|know}} Me, from the least of them to the greatest, declares the LORD. For I will forgive their iniquities and will remember their sins no more.”

units.label: Fixed as creation, a holy city rebuilt

verses.text: Thus says the LORD, who gives the sun for light by day, who sets in order the moon and stars for light by night, who stirs up the sea so that its waves roar, the LORD of Hosts is His name:
“Only if this fixed order departed from My presence, declares the LORD, would Israel's descendants ever cease to be a nation before Me.”

This is what the LORD says: 
“Only if the heavens above could be measured and the foundations of the earth below searched out would I reject all of Israel's descendants because of all they have done,” declares the LORD.
“The days are coming,” declares the LORD, “when this city will be rebuilt for Me, from the tower of Hananel to the Corner Gate.
The measuring line will once again stretch out straight to the hill of Gareb and then turn toward Goah.

The whole valley of the dead bodies and ashes, and all the fields as far as the Kidron Valley, to the corner of the Horse Gate to the east, will be holy to the LORD. It will never again be uprooted or demolished.”

ol.gloss: A yoke, the wooden crossbar of imposed servitude. Judah had been told to wear Babylon's yoke as God's appointed discipline, Jeremiah 27 to 28; here God promises the day is coming when He Himself will break that yoke off His people's necks.

ahavatOlam.gloss: Everlasting love, love without end. God grounds the whole restoration not in Israel's worthiness but in a love that reaches back before their sin and forward past their exile, the reason He keeps drawing His people back to Himself.

beritChadashah.gloss: New covenant. Unlike the Sinai covenant, external and broken, this covenant places God's law within His people and forgives their sin completely; the New Testament identifies this as the covenant Christ inaugurates in His blood, Luke 22:20, Hebrews 8:8-12.

yada.gloss: To know, intimately and experientially, not merely intellectually. Under the new covenant every one of God's people will yada Him directly, from the least to the greatest, no longer dependent on another to teach them who He is.

basic.context: In the middle of oracles of judgment, Jeremiah is commanded to write down an entire book of consolation. Jacob's coming distress will end in salvation, the incurable wound will be healed, the ruined city rebuilt, Rachel's weeping turned to joy, and, at the center of it all, God promises a new covenant unlike the one broken at Sinai: His law written on hearts, not tablets, and sins remembered no more.
basic.hermeneutics: This describes a real, future restoration for God's exiled people, one whose language of gathering, rebuilding, and covenant renewal the New Testament explicitly takes up. The new covenant of 31:31-34 is not a vague spiritual metaphor; Jesus quotes its very language over the cup at the Last Supper, Luke 22:20, and Hebrews quotes the whole passage to explain the difference Christ's blood makes.
basic.originalLanguages: God's restoration flows from an ahavat olam, an everlasting love, that predates and outlasts Israel's unfaithfulness. It climaxes in a berit chadashah, a new covenant, in which God's people will yada Him, know Him directly and intimately, rather than depending on another to teach them, because their sins are forgiven and remembered no more.
basic.typology: The broken yoke anticipates the true freedom Christ offers, Matthew 11:29-30. The healing of an incurable wound anticipates the atonement that heals what nothing else could, 1 Peter 2:24. Above all, the new covenant of the heart anticipates and is directly named as fulfilled in Christ's blood, poured out for many, Luke 22:20, Hebrews 8:8-12.
basic.christ: Jeremiah 31:31-34 is the Old Testament's clearest promise of the new covenant, and the New Testament repeatedly identifies Jesus as the one who establishes it. At the Last Supper He takes the cup and says, this cup is the new covenant in My blood, Luke 22:20, deliberately using Jeremiah's own words. Hebrews 8:8-12 quotes this passage at length to show that Christ's priesthood and sacrifice have made the first covenant obsolete.
basic.god: God disciplines His people justly for real sin, yet His anger is not His last word; His everlasting love guarantees He will heal, rebuild, and forgive completely. He is as reliable in His covenant faithfulness as the fixed order of the sun, moon, and sea, so certain that only the undoing of creation itself could undo His promise to His people.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 22:20 records Jesus naming the cup of the Last Supper as the new covenant in My blood, directly invoking this passage. Hebrews 8:8-12 quotes Jeremiah 31:31-34 in full to explain Christ's superior priesthood. Matthew 2:17-18 applies Rachel's weeping in 31:15 to the slaughter of the infants at Bethlehem. Matthew 11:28-30 answers the broken yoke of 30:8 with Christ's own easy yoke.
basic.oneStory: The Book of Consolation promises that God's discipline of His exiled people will not be His last word: He will heal, rebuild, and gather them, and above all make a new covenant that changes hearts and forgives sin completely. That covenant is not a future hope deferred forever; the New Testament shows Jesus establishing it in His own blood, so that everyone who trusts Him already lives under the new covenant Jeremiah foresaw.

context.literary: Often called the Book of Consolation, these two chapters form a self-contained collection of hope oracles set as a deliberate hinge in the middle of Jeremiah's largely judgment-heavy prophecies, moving from poetry describing the day of Jacob's trouble to the prose climax of the new covenant and back to poetry describing the rebuilt city.
context.historical: Delivered while Jerusalem's fall to Babylon was imminent or already underway, these chapters gave the exiled and about-to-be-exiled community a hope that reached beyond the immediate catastrophe to a genuinely future, national and covenantal restoration.
context.canonical: Jeremiah 31:31-34 is the only place in the Old Testament where the phrase new covenant appears explicitly, making it the direct verbal source for the New Testament's own name for itself and for Christ's words at the Last Supper.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure God's disciplined, exiled people that His judgment is not His final purpose for them, and to reveal, at the center of that assurance, a coming covenant that will succeed where the Sinai covenant failed, by changing the heart itself and forgiving sin completely.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is prophetic promise, not merely descriptive history or a list of commands; it describes what God will certainly do. The New Testament treats its central promise, the new covenant, as already inaugurated in Christ, so the passage functions as fulfilled prophecy for the church rather than a still-future program alone.

debates.question: Is the new covenant of Jeremiah 31:31-34 already fulfilled in the church, or does it await a future, distinct fulfillment for ethnic Israel?

views.label: Fulfilled now in the church through Christ
views.case: Hebrews 8:8-12 quotes this passage to describe what Christ has already accomplished, and Jesus applies its language directly to His own blood at the Last Supper, Luke 22:20, so all who trust Christ, Jew and Gentile, already live under this new covenant.

views.label: Inaugurated in Christ, with a further national fulfillment for Israel still to come
views.case: The passage explicitly names the house of Israel and the house of Judah, and Paul anticipates a future turning of ethnic Israel to Christ, Romans 11:26-27, suggesting the new covenant's blessings, already available in Christ, will be embraced nationally by Israel in a fuller way yet to come.

debates.bearing: Both views agree the new covenant is established in Christ's blood and already available to all who trust Him; they differ only on whether a further, specifically national fulfillment for Israel remains, a question that does not change how the covenant is entered now, through faith in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The whole promise rests on God's ahavat olam, His everlasting love, the reason He keeps returning to a people who kept breaking His covenant. It reaches its center in the berit chadashah, the new covenant, whose distinguishing mark is that God's people will yada Him, know Him personally and directly, because their sins are forgiven and remembered no more, not merely covered as at Sinai but truly removed.

typology.type: The broken yoke of Babylon, Jeremiah 30:8
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the true and lasting freedom Christ offers His people, whose yoke alone is easy and whose burden alone is light, Matthew 11:29-30.

typology.type: The new covenant written on the heart, Jeremiah 31:31-34
typology.fulfillment: Directly named and inaugurated by Christ at the Last Supper, this cup is the new covenant in My blood, Luke 22:20, and expounded at length in Hebrews 8:8-12 as fulfilled in His priesthood and sacrifice.

study.christ: Jeremiah 31:31-34 is the summit of the Book of Consolation and one of the most directly fulfilled prophecies in all of Scripture. Jesus takes up its very words at the Last Supper, this cup that is poured out for you is the new covenant in My blood, Luke 22:20, and the author of Hebrews quotes the passage in full to show that Christ's single sacrifice has accomplished what the old covenant, written on stone and repeatedly broken, never could, a law written on the heart and sins remembered no more, Hebrews 8:8-12, 10:16-17.
study.god: God disciplines His people justly, this whole book has shown that His anger over sin is real, yet His everlasting love, not His anger, is the deeper and final truth about Him. He binds Himself to a promise as unbreakable as the fixed order of sun, moon, and sea, and He is the one who will do the internal, heart-level work that mere law could never accomplish, forgiving sin so completely it is remembered no more.

crossReferences.note: Jesus names the cup at the Last Supper the new covenant in My blood, directly invoking Jeremiah's own language.
Quotes Jeremiah 31:31-34 at length to show Christ's priesthood and sacrifice have inaugurated exactly this promised covenant.
Applies Rachel's weeping for her children, Jeremiah 31:15, to the slaughter of the infants at Bethlehem, and by extension to the hope of comfort beyond it.
Christ's easy yoke directly answers the broken yoke of bondage promised in Jeremiah 30:8.

study.oneStory: The Book of Consolation promises that God's just discipline of His exiled people is not His last word: He will heal, rebuild, and gather them, and at the center of that promise make a new covenant that changes the heart itself and forgives sin completely. The New Testament shows this is no longer a distant hope, Christ has already inaugurated the new covenant in His own blood, so that everyone who trusts Him now lives under the very promise Jeremiah wrote down in the Book of Consolation.

sermon.title: Christmas at the Castle - Part 4
sermon.why: Carson preaches directly through Jeremiah 30-31, tracing the movement from the day of Jacob's trouble to the new covenant promise, and shows how the whole Book of Consolation finds its fulfillment in Christ.

quiz.q: What is the day of Jacob's trouble in Jeremiah 30:7, and what is its ultimate outcome?

options.text: A time of unmatched distress for God's people, out of which He promises they will be saved.
options.note: Verse 7 explicitly pairs the severity of the distress with the promise that Jacob will be saved out of it.

options.text: A day of final, total destruction with no promise of survival.
options.note: The verse explicitly promises that Jacob will be saved out of this distress, not destroyed by it.

options.text: A minor, brief inconvenience for the exiles.
options.note: The text describes it as unmatched distress, none will be like it, not something minor.

options.text: A day that only affects the surrounding nations, not Israel.
options.note: The distress is specifically named as Jacob's own trouble, though surrounding nations are addressed elsewhere in the passage.

quiz.q: What does God promise regarding Rachel's weeping in Jeremiah 31:15-17?

options.text: That her weeping will be answered with hope, for her children will return from the land of the enemy.
options.note: Verses 16 to 17 directly answer the weeping of verse 15 with the promise of the children's return.

options.text: That the weeping is final and will never be comforted.
options.note: The text explicitly commands, keep your voice from weeping, because comfort and return are promised.

options.text: That Rachel herself would be resurrected immediately.
options.note: The promise concerns the return of her children, the exiled people, not a literal resurrection of Rachel.

options.text: That the weeping refers only to a single, unrelated historical event with no future promise attached.
options.note: The weeping is directly tied to the specific promise of hope and return in the same passage.

quiz.q: According to Jeremiah 31:31-34, what makes the new covenant different from the covenant made at the exodus?

options.text: God will put His law within His people and write it on their hearts, and He will forgive their sins completely, remembering them no more.
options.note: Verses 33 to 34 name both the internal transformation and the complete forgiveness as the new covenant's distinguishing marks.

options.text: It requires stricter external rules than the covenant made at Sinai.
options.note: The new covenant is marked by internal transformation, not additional external rules.

options.text: It is made only with a small remnant and excludes the rest of Israel and Judah.
options.note: The text explicitly names both the house of Israel and the house of Judah as recipients.

options.text: It is identical in every respect to the covenant made at the exodus.
options.note: Verse 32 explicitly contrasts it with that covenant, which Israel broke; it is not identical.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament identify the fulfillment of the new covenant promised in Jeremiah 31?

options.text: Jesus names the cup at the Last Supper the new covenant in His blood, and Hebrews quotes the passage to show it fulfilled in Christ's priesthood and sacrifice.
options.note: Luke 22:20 and Hebrews 8:8-12 both directly connect this promise to Christ's own words and work.

options.text: The New Testament never references this promise.
options.note: Luke 22:20 and Hebrews 8:8-12 both directly quote or invoke this exact passage.

options.text: It is fulfilled only in a future political kingdom with no connection to Christ's death.
options.note: The New Testament ties the new covenant directly to Christ's blood and sacrifice, not merely a future political arrangement.

options.text: It is treated as a minor, symbolic reference with no bearing on the sacraments.
options.note: Jesus applies this promise directly to the cup of the Last Supper, giving it central sacramental significance.

quiz.q: What guarantees the certainty of God's covenant faithfulness in Jeremiah 31:35-37?

options.text: It is compared to the fixed order of the sun, moon, stars, and sea, as unbreakable as creation itself.
options.note: Verses 35 to 37 explicitly ground God's covenant faithfulness in the fixed, unchangeable order of creation.

options.text: It depends entirely on Israel's own future faithfulness.
options.note: The passage grounds the promise in God's fixed created order, not in Israel's own performance.

options.text: It is left uncertain and conditional throughout the passage.
options.note: The passage presents the promise as certain, comparing it to the fixed order of creation, not as uncertain.

options.text: It is guaranteed by the continued existence of the Babylonian empire.
options.note: The guarantee comes from God's own created order, not from any human empire's continuation.

questions: Jacob's distress was real and severe, yet God promised he would be saved out of it, not spared from it entirely. Where are you needing to trust that God will bring you through hardship rather than simply remove it?
God says of Zion, they call you an outcast, for whom no one cares, but I will restore your health. Where do you feel like an outcast, and how does it change things to know God cares even there?
The new covenant promises God's law written on the heart, not merely known in the mind. Where is there a gap between what you know is true and what actually governs your heart and affections?
Under the new covenant, sins are forgiven and remembered no more. Is there a sin you have confessed but still cannot believe God has truly forgotten?
God's faithfulness is compared to the fixed order of sun, moon, and sea. Where do you need that same steady confidence in God's promises this week?

translationNotes.note: The time of Jacob's distress became a phrase later applied in Jewish and Christian interpretation to a final period of tribulation before decisive deliverance, though in its immediate context it refers first to the distress of exile itself.

Ramah was near the traditional site of Rachel's tomb and a gathering point for exiles being marched to Babylon, giving the image of Rachel weeping for her children a vivid, geographically specific setting.

This verse contains the only occurrence of the phrase new covenant in the entire Old Testament, and it is quoted in full in Hebrews 8:8-12, making it one of the most directly quoted Old Testament passages in the New Testament.
